items.question: Who uses likelihood functions in everyday ML work?
items.answer: ML engineers and data scientists use L(θ) implicitly whenever they minimize cross-entropy or NLL, fit MLE parameters, or build generative classifiers—any pipeline that asks which θ best explains observed training data.

items.question: What is the likelihood function L(θ)?
items.answer: L(θ) = f(x | θ) with observed data x fixed and parameter θ varying; it scores how compatible each θ is with the data using the same PMF/PDF as the probability model.

items.question: When do you multiply likelihood factors into a product?
items.answer: When observations are modeled as independent and identically distributed from the same f(x | θ); then L(θ) = ∏_i f(x_i | θ), equivalently ℓ(θ) = Σ_i log f(x_i | θ).

items.question: Where does likelihood appear in Bayes' theorem?
items.answer: As the data term P(data | θ), i.e. L(θ): posterior ∝ prior × likelihood. Without a prior, likelihood alone supports MLE but not P(θ | data).

items.question: Why take the logarithm of the likelihood?
items.answer: log is monotonic (same maximizer), converts products to sums for easier derivatives and mini-batch averaging, and prevents numerical underflow when many small probability factors multiply.

items.question: How does likelihood differ from probability in practice?
items.answer: Fix θ and vary x for probability (normalized over x); fix observed x and vary θ for likelihood (not normalized over θ). Same formula, swapped roles and interpretation.

items.question: Likelihood L(θ) vs posterior P(θ | x): what differs?
items.answer: L(θ) = f(x | θ) uses only the model and data; P(θ | x) ∝ P(θ)·L(θ) also requires a prior over θ. MLE uses L alone; Bayes combines prior and likelihood.

items.question: L(θ) vs ℓ(θ): when to use which?
items.answer: They share the same argmax. Use L(θ) for intuition and likelihood ratios; use ℓ(θ) or negative ℓ(θ) in code, gradients, and sums over datasets because products underflow and logs linearize optimization.

items.question: Joint likelihood vs single-factor likelihood?
items.answer: Single-factor L(θ) = f(x | θ) is one observation; joint likelihood is the product (or log-sum) over many IID observations. Training loss is average negative log of the joint factorization.

items.question: Likelihood vs loss function naming in deep learning?
items.answer: Cross-entropy and NLL are negative log-likelihoods under stated models; 'loss' emphasizes minimization, 'likelihood' emphasizes the probabilistic generative story—they refer to the same objective up to sign and scaling.

items.question: What happens when you maximize L(θ) for Bernoulli data with k successes in n trials?
items.answer: Cause: L(p) = p^k(1−p)^{n−k} is maximized over p ∈ [0,1]. Effect: MLE p̂ = k/n—the sample proportion explains the data best under the Bernoulli model.

items.question: What is the effect of using the wrong f(x | θ) in the likelihood?
items.answer: Cause: misspecified PMF/PDF (e.g. Gaussian on count data). Effect: you still get a θ̂ that maximizes the wrong L(θ), often yielding biased fit, poor calibration, and misleading comparisons to other models.
